1: Chapter 26 is the narrative counterpart to the Temple Sermon of chapter 7: where chapter 7 gives the content of the sermon, chapter 26 gives the story of what happened when Jeremiah delivered it. In the beginning of the reign of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah, this word came from YHWH. The dating to Jehoiakim's accession year (608/607 BC) places this shortly after Josiah's death at Megiddo and Jehoahaz's brief reign followed by Egyptian imposition of Jehoiakim. The political context matters: Jehoiakim was an Egyptian puppet, hostile to the reform traditions of his father Josiah, and deeply opposed to Jeremiah's prophetic message.
2: Thus says YHWH: Stand in the court of YHWH's house, and speak to all the cities of Judah that come to worship in the house of YHWH all the words that I command you to speak to them; do not hold back a word. The command do not hold back a word (al tigra' davar) is both a commission and a warning: the completeness of the message is non-negotiable. Any softening of the oracle would constitute disobedience. The Temple court is the most public possible location — pilgrims from all Judah's cities gather there, making it the ideal place for a message to the entire nation.
3: It may be they will listen, and every one turn from his evil way, that I may relent of the disaster that I intend to do to them because of their evil deeds. The perhaps (ulai) maintains the possibility of repentance even while the judgment is being announced. The divine flexibility established in chapter 18 (if the nation repents, I relent) is operative here: the sermon is not a mechanical announcement of inevitable doom but a genuine call to repentance with real potential for course-correction. YHWH's sovereign freedom to relent means the prophetic word is never simply fatalistic.
4: You shall say to them, Thus says YHWH: If you will not listen to me, to walk in my law that I have set before you. The conditional form: covenant obedience (walking in YHWH's Torah) is the prerequisite for covenant blessing and protection. The failure to listen (shama) and walk (halak) — hearing and living — is the two-part covenant failure that Jeremiah diagnoses throughout the book.
5: And to listen to the words of my servants the prophets whom I send to you urgently, though you have not listened. The servants-the-prophets formula recurs (cf. Jeremiah 7:25; Jeremiah 25:4): YHWH has maintained a continuous succession of covenant messengers, sent urgently and persistently. The though you have not listened is the historical record embedded in the invitation — the people's past track record makes the outcome predictable, but the invitation remains genuine.
6: Then I will make this house like Shiloh, and I will make this city a curse for all the nations of the earth. The Shiloh comparison is the theological bomb of the Temple Sermon: Shiloh was the original location of the Tabernacle and the Ark before the Philistines captured the Ark (1 Sam 4–6). Shiloh was destroyed — archaeologically confirmed by a destruction layer from the late 11th century BC. If YHWH abandoned Shiloh, the place of his first dwelling among Israel, he can abandon Jerusalem too. The inviolability theology that protected the false prophets' peace-message is demolished by historical precedent.
7: The priests and the prophets and all the people heard Jeremiah speaking these words in the house of YHWH. The audience is precisely those who have the most to lose from Jeremiah's message: the priests (Temple establishment), the (false) prophets (who have been announcing peace), and the general population (who have been reassured by the false prophets). All three groups hear the Shiloh comparison simultaneously.
8: And when Jeremiah had finished speaking all that YHWH had commanded him to speak to all the people, then the priests and the prophets and all the people laid hold of him, saying, You shall die! The institutional response is immediate violence: you shall die (mot tamut). The death-penalty formula is the legal language of capital punishment. The charge will be prophesying against the Temple and the city (v11) — using Jeremiah's own words as the indictment. False prophesying against a city in the ancient Near East was a capital offense; the priests and false prophets believe they have a legitimate legal basis for execution.
9: Why have you prophesied in the name of YHWH, saying, This house shall be like Shiloh, and this city shall be desolate, without inhabitant? And all the people gathered around Jeremiah in the house of YHWH. The accusation quotes Jeremiah's words precisely. The gathering of the people in the Temple court is a proto-judicial assembly — in the ancient Near East, major legal cases were heard in public spaces where the community could witness. The case against Jeremiah will be decided by this assembly.
10: When the officials of Judah heard these things, they came up from the king's house to the house of YHWH and took their seat in the entry of the New Gate of the house of YHWH. The officials (sarim) come from the palace to hold a formal hearing at the Temple gate — the standard ancient Near Eastern location for legal proceedings. Their arrival shifts the proceeding from a mob threat to a formal judicial process, which paradoxically increases Jeremiah's chances of survival: mob violence might have killed him immediately; a formal trial gives him the opportunity to speak in his defense.
11: Then the priests and the prophets said to the officials and to all the people, This man deserves the sentence of death, because he has prophesied against this city, as you have heard with your own ears. The prosecution presents its case: Jeremiah has committed the capital offense of prophesying against the city. The charge is technically accurate — he did prophesy that the city would be like Shiloh. But the prosecution has confused the form of the message (prophecy of doom) with false prophecy. Jeremiah's message was authorized by YHWH; the false prophets' message was not.
12: Then Jeremiah spoke to all the officials and all the people, saying, YHWH sent me to prophesy against this house and this city all the words you have heard. Jeremiah's defense is simple and direct: YHWH sent me (YHWH shalachani). The sent/not-sent criterion that distinguishes true from false prophecy (cf. Jeremiah 14:14; Jeremiah 23:21; Jeremiah 27:15; Jeremiah 28:15) is now Jeremiah's legal defense. If YHWH sent him, executing him is murdering YHWH's messenger — a capital crime itself.
13: Now therefore mend your ways and your deeds, and obey the voice of YHWH your God, and YHWH will relent of the disaster that he has pronounced against you. Jeremiah uses his own trial as an opportunity to deliver the core message again: repentance produces divine relenting. Even in the midst of being threatened with death, the prophet offers the path to life. This is the pastoral courage of genuine prophecy — the announcement of judgment is always in service of the call to repentance.
14: But as for me, behold, I am in your hands. Do with me as seems good and right to you. The remarkable surrender: Jeremiah places himself entirely in the hands of the court. He does not resist, flee, or compromise the message to save himself. The willingness to die for the message is itself a testimony to its authenticity — a false prophet would recant under threat of death. Jeremiah's acceptance of the court's authority while refusing to alter his message models the prophetic integrity that the book has been teaching throughout.
15: Only know for certain that if you put me to death, you will bring innocent blood upon yourselves and upon this city and its inhabitants, for in truth YHWH sent me to you to speak all these words in your ears. The counter-charge: executing Jeremiah will bring innocent blood (dam naqui) on the city. The shedding of innocent blood was itself a capital sin (Deuteronomy 19:10–13; 2 Kings 24:4) and a source of covenant pollution. Jeremiah is warning the court that killing him will not avert judgment but will add a new capital crime to the existing list of covenant violations.
16: Then the officials and all the people said to the priests and the prophets, This man does not deserve the sentence of death, for he has spoken to us in the name of YHWH our God. The verdict: acquittal. The officials and people reverse the verdict of the priests and prophets. The theological reason given is precisely the criterion of true prophecy: he has spoken in the name of YHWH our God. The name of YHWH functions as the authorization that distinguishes true from false prophecy. The crowd recognizes something genuine in Jeremiah's bearing and message that the professional religious establishment was suppressing.
17: And certain of the elders of the land arose and spoke to all the assembled people, saying. The elders provide historical precedent — the wisdom tradition of the community. In the ancient Near East, elders were the keepers of historical memory; their invocation of past cases established legal precedent. The elders' intervention introduces a crucial historical argument that will shift the case decisively.
18: Micah of Moresheth prophesied in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, and said to all the people of Judah: Thus says YHWH of hosts, 'Zion shall be plowed as a field; Jerusalem shall become a heap of ruins, and the mountain of the house a wooded height'. The elders cite Micah 3:12 — one of the most famous doom-prophecies in the prophetic tradition. Micah prophesied the destruction of Jerusalem in the late 8th century BC (contemporary with Isaiah). His message was the same as Jeremiah's: Jerusalem will be destroyed because of covenant violation.
19: Did Hezekiah king of Judah and all Judah put him to death? Did he not fear YHWH and entreat the favor of YHWH, and did not YHWH relent of the disaster that he had pronounced against them? But we are about to bring great disaster upon ourselves. Hezekiah's response to Micah becomes the legal precedent: the king repented (feared YHWH and entreated his favor) and YHWH relented. Executing Micah would have been a capital crime; Hezekiah wisely accepted the prophetic word. The implication for Jeremiah's case is clear: repeating Hezekiah's wisdom (repentance, not execution) will produce the same result (divine relenting). Repeating the opposite will produce the opposite.
20: There was another man who prophesied in the name of YHWH, Uriah the son of Shemaiah from Kiriath-jearim. He prophesied against this city and against this land in words like those of Jeremiah. The counter-example: Uriah, a contemporary of Jeremiah who also prophesied doom against Jerusalem and Judah. Uriah's case represents the worst-case scenario — what happens when the king executes a true prophet. The detail that he prophesied in words like those of Jeremiah confirms that Jeremiah was not speaking an unprecedented or aberrant message but a consistent covenant warning.
21: And when King Jehoiakim, with all his warriors and all the officials, heard his words, the king sought to put him to death. But when Uriah heard of it, he was afraid and fled and escaped to Egypt. Uriah's fear led him to flee to Egypt — the typical destination for Judahite political refugees. But Egypt was no sanctuary from a king who had Pharaoh Neco's support. The flight to Egypt is presented as human prudence overriding prophetic commitment.
22: Then King Jehoiakim sent to Egypt certain men, Elnathan the son of Achbor and others with him, and they took Uriah from Egypt and brought him to King Jehoiakim, who struck him down with the sword and dumped his body in the burial place of the common people. Uriah was extradited from Egypt and executed — the donkey-burial threat of 22:19 enacted on a lesser-known prophet before Jehoiakim. The burial place of the common people is a dishonorable mass grave, not a family tomb — the maximum available indignity for the executed. The contrast with Jeremiah's acquittal is deliberate: Uriah fled and was executed; Jeremiah stayed and was acquitted. Faithfulness in the face of threat produced a better outcome than flight.
23: Verse 23 summarizes the Uriah incident already described: his extradition, execution, and dishonorable burial. The narrative detail underlines the political reality of Jehoiakim's reign — this is a king who killed prophets. Jeremiah's survival is therefore all the more remarkable and requires the explanation provided in the next verse.
24: But the hand of Ahikam the son of Shaphan was with Jeremiah so that he was not given over to the people to be put to death. Ahikam ben Shaphan — a prominent court official whose family will appear throughout Jeremiah. Shaphan was the scribe who read the law-scroll to Hezekiah (2 Kings 22:8–14) during Josiah's reforms; Ahikam was part of the delegation sent to Huldah the prophetess (2 Kings 22:12–14); Gedaliah ben Ahikam will become the governor of Judah after Jerusalem's fall (Jeremiah 40:5). The Shaphan family represents a thread of covenant fidelity running through the political establishment — a remnant of faithful officials who protected YHWH's prophet.
